annotations.label: Cardinality

anchor.snippet: Cardinality is an inherent property of sets which defines their size

annotations.note: `Cardinal.mk α` (notation `#α`) is exactly the cardinality of a type/set.

annotations.label: Comparing sets by one-to-one correspondence

anchor.snippet: cardinality is measured by putting sets in one-to-one correspondence

annotations.note: `Cardinal.eq : #α = #β ↔ Nonempty (α ≃ β)` defines equal size by one-to-one correspondence.

annotations.label: Set

anchor.snippet: The only requirement for a set is that it is well-defined

annotations.note: `Set α := α → Prop` is Mathlib's notion of a set of elements of a type.

annotations.label: Set of natural numbers (infinite set)

anchor.snippet: One example of an infinite set is the set of all natural numbers

annotations.note: ℕ is the prototypical infinite set, with `Cardinal.aleph0 = #ℕ` and an `Infinite ℕ` instance.

annotations.label: Function

anchor.snippet: A function , or correspondence, maps members of one set to the members of another

annotations.note: Functions are the primitive dependent/non-dependent arrow (`α → β`) type of Lean's type theory.

annotations.label: Injective function

anchor.snippet: If a function does not map two members to the same place, it is called injective

annotations.note: `Function.Injective` is the standard definition of an injective function.

annotations.label: Surjective function

anchor.snippet: If a function covers every member in the output set, it is called surjective

annotations.note: `Function.Surjective` is the standard definition of a surjective function.

annotations.label: Bijective function

anchor.snippet: If a function is both injective and surjective, it is called bijective

annotations.note: `Function.Bijective f := Injective f ∧ Surjective f` matches exactly.

annotations.label: Function multiplying by two

anchor.snippet: one may define a function on the natural numbers by multiplying by two

annotations.note: An illustrative ad-hoc function with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Equinumerous / same cardinality

anchor.snippet: Two sets are said to have the same cardinality or be equinumerous if their members can be paired one-to-one

annotations.note: Equinumerosity is `Nonempty (α ≃ β)`, equivalent to `#α = #β` by `Cardinal.eq`.

annotations.label: Even numbers equinumerous with naturals

anchor.snippet: of even numbers has the same cardinality as the set

annotations.note: Follows from any infinite subset of ℕ being denumerable, but there is no named lemma for the even numbers specifically.

annotations.label: Whole-greater-than-part fails for infinite sets

anchor.snippet: The property for finite sets that "the whole is greater than the part" is no longer true for infinite sets

annotations.note: Mathlib captures the finite case (injective self-map is surjective); the infinite-set failure is its negation but isn't a standalone lemma.

annotations.label: Equinumerosity is an equivalence relation

anchor.snippet: equinumerosity forms an equivalence relation

annotations.note: Equinumerosity equals equality of `Cardinal.mk` (and `Equiv.refl/symm/trans` give the reflexive/symmetric/transitive structure).

annotations.label: Cardinal inequality (≤)

anchor.snippet: is less than or equal to the cardinality of

annotations.note: `Cardinal.le_def : #α ≤ #β ↔ Nonempty (α ↪ β)` is inequality via injection.

annotations.label: Strictly smaller cardinality (<)

anchor.snippet: is said to be strictly smaller than

annotations.note: `<` is the strict order induced by the `≤` of `Cardinal.le_def` on the linearly-ordered `Cardinal`.

annotations.label: Four-element and five-element sets

anchor.snippet: has four elements and

annotations.note: Finite sizes are computed by `Fintype.card`; this particular 4 < 5 comparison is just an example.

annotations.label: Cardinal inequality is reflexive and transitive

anchor.snippet: as defined above is reflexive since the identity function is injective, and is transitive by function composition

annotations.note: `Cardinal` is a (linear) order, so `≤` is reflexive and transitive via its order instance.

annotations.label: Schröder–Bernstein theorem

anchor.snippet: is the content of the so-called Schröder–Bernstein theorem

annotations.note: `schroeder_bernstein` gives a bijection from mutual injections, matching the theorem.

annotations.label: Order type of a well-ordered set

anchor.snippet: Every well-ordered set is isomorphic to a unique ordinal number , called the order type of the set

annotations.note: `Ordinal.type r` is the order type of a well-order, defined as the quotient identifying order-isomorphic well-orders.

annotations.label: Well-ordering theorem

anchor.snippet: By the well-ordering theorem , every set can be well-ordered

annotations.note: `WellOrderingRel` plus its `IsWellOrder` instance well-orders any type (using choice).

annotations.label: Totality/comparability of cardinals (equivalent to AC)

anchor.snippet: A further property of cardinal inequality is totality , which says that any two sets are comparable

annotations.note: Comparability holds since `Cardinal` is a `LinearOrder` (relying on choice), but the equivalence with AC is not formalized.

annotations.label: Countable / countably infinite set

anchor.snippet: A set is called countable if it is finite or has a bijection with the set of natural numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Countable` (injects into ℕ) covers both finite and countably-infinite, equivalent to the article's definition.

annotations.label: Even natural numbers are countable

anchor.snippet: the set of all even natural numbers is countable

annotations.note: Any subset of the countable type ℕ is `Set.Countable`.

annotations.label: Square numbers are countable

anchor.snippet: the set of square numbers is countable

annotations.note: The squares are a subset of countable ℕ, hence `Set.Countable`.

annotations.label: Rational numbers

anchor.snippet: are those which can be expressed as the quotient or fraction

annotations.note: `Rat` (ℚ) is the type of rationals as reduced numerator/denominator fractions.

annotations.label: Rational numbers are countable

anchor.snippet: The rational numbers can be shown to be countable by considering the set of fractions

annotations.note: `Cardinal.mkRat : #ℚ = ℵ₀` states the rationals are countably infinite.

annotations.label: Algebraic number

anchor.snippet: A number is called algebraic if it is a solution of some polynomial equation

annotations.note: `IsAlgebraic R x` means `x` is a root of a nonzero polynomial over `R`.

annotations.label: Transcendental number

anchor.snippet: a number which cannot be the root of any polynomial is called transcendental

annotations.note: `Transcendental R x := ¬ IsAlgebraic R x` matches the definition.

annotations.label: Set of algebraic numbers is countable

anchor.snippet: it can be shown that the set of algebraic numbers is countable by ordering the polynomials lexicographically

annotations.note: `Algebraic.countable : Set.Countable {x | IsAlgebraic R x}` for a countable base ring.

annotations.label: Almost all real numbers are transcendental

anchor.snippet: almost all real numbers are transcendental

annotations.note: Algebraics are countable and ℝ uncountable, so transcendentals are co-countable, but no explicit 'almost all' statement is recorded.

annotations.label: Hilbert's Grand Hotel

anchor.snippet: Hilbert's paradox of the Grand Hotel is a popular thought experiment

annotations.note: A pedagogical thought experiment with no formal Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Uncountable set

anchor.snippet: A set is called uncountable if it is not countable

annotations.note: `Uncountable α` is defined as not `Countable α`.

annotations.label: Real numbers are uncountable (diagonal argument)

anchor.snippet: One method of proving that the reals are uncountable is called Cantor's diagonal argument

annotations.note: `Cardinal.not_countable_real` proves ℝ is uncountable (via cardinality of the continuum rather than diagonalization).

annotations.label: Power set of the naturals is uncountable

anchor.snippet: Another classical example of an uncountable set, established using a related reasoning, is the power set of the natural numbers

annotations.note: `mk_set_nat : #(Set ℕ) = 𝔠`, which exceeds ℵ₀ by `aleph0_lt_continuum`, so P(ℕ) is uncountable.

annotations.label: ℝ and P(ℕ) equinumerous (cardinality of the continuum)

anchor.snippet: can be shown to have the same cardinality

annotations.note: `mk_real : #ℝ = 𝔠` and `mk_set_nat : #(Set ℕ) = 𝔠` give `#ℝ = #(Set ℕ)`.

annotations.label: Cantor's theorem

anchor.snippet: Cantor's theorem generalizes the second theorem above, showing that every set is strictly smaller than its powerset

annotations.note: `Cardinal.cantor : a < 2 ^ a` is Cantor's theorem (also `Function.cantor_surjective`).

annotations.label: Infinite hierarchy of infinities

anchor.snippet: Repeating this argument shows that there are infinitely many "sizes" of infinity

annotations.note: Iterating `Cardinal.cantor` gives `NoMaxOrder Cardinal`, i.e. unboundedly many cardinalities.

annotations.label: Cardinal number

anchor.snippet: This number is called the cardinal number of that set, or simply the cardinality of that set

annotations.note: `Cardinal` is the type of cardinal numbers (quotient of types by equinumerosity).

annotations.label: Axiom of cardinality / Hume's principle

anchor.snippet: sometimes called the axiom of cardinality

annotations.note: `Cardinal.eq : #α = #β ↔ Nonempty (α ≃ β)` is exactly Hume's principle.

annotations.label: Frege–Russell cardinal numbers

anchor.snippet: These called the Frege–Russell cardinal numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Cardinal` is a quotient of a type universe rather than the Frege–Russell class of all equinumerous sets.

annotations.label: Finite cardinality (cardinality n)

anchor.snippet: Given a basic sense of natural numbers , a set is said to have cardinality

annotations.note: `Nat.card α` gives the finite cardinality of `α` as a natural number.

annotations.label: Set with cardinality 4

anchor.snippet: and therefore is said to have cardinality 4

annotations.note: A specific set of size 4 is an instance of `Fintype.card`/`Nat.card` equal to 4.

annotations.label: Peano axioms

anchor.snippet: the natural numbers can be understood as some construction of objects satisfying the Peano axioms

annotations.note: ℕ realizes the Peano axioms via its inductive definition and recursor, but they are not bundled as an explicit axiom structure.

annotations.label: Finite cardinality corresponds uniquely with naturals

anchor.snippet: finite cardinality corresponds uniquely with natural numbers

annotations.note: `Nat.card` assigns each finite set a unique natural number (injectivity of `ℕ → Cardinal`).

annotations.label: Pigeonhole principle

anchor.snippet: is equivalent to a certain formulation of the pigeonhole principle , that a finite set cannot be put in one to one correspondence with a proper subset of itself

annotations.note: For a finite type an injective self-map is surjective, so it cannot biject with a proper subset.

annotations.label: Addition principle

anchor.snippet: The addition principle asserts that given disjoint sets

annotations.note: `add_def : #α + #β = #(α ⊕ β)` formalizes adding cardinalities of disjoint sets.

annotations.label: Multiplication principle

anchor.snippet: The multiplication principle asserts that given two sets

annotations.note: `mk_prod : #(α × β) = #α * #β` formalizes the multiplication principle.

annotations.label: Inclusion–exclusion principle

anchor.snippet: The more general result is the inclusion–exclusion principle , which defines how to count the number of elements in overlapping sets

annotations.note: `Finset.inclusion_exclusion_card_biUnion` is the inclusion–exclusion formula for cardinalities.

annotations.label: Finite set

anchor.snippet: a set is defined to be finite if it is empty or can be put in correspondence with the set

annotations.note: `Finite α` means `α` is in bijection with some `Fin n`, equivalent to the article's definition.

annotations.label: Dedekind-finite set

anchor.snippet: a set is called Dedekind-finite if it cannot be put in one-to-one correspondence with a proper subset of itself

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated set-theoretic Dedekind-finite predicate; its equivalence with `Finite` is witnessed by `injective_iff_surjective`.

annotations.label: Aleph numbers

anchor.snippet: The aleph numbers are a sequence of cardinal numbers that represent the sizes of infinite sets

annotations.note: `Cardinal.aleph` is the order-embedding enumerating the infinite cardinals.

annotations.label: Aleph-nought (ℵ₀)

anchor.snippet: called "aleph-nought", "aleph-zero", or "aleph-null", which represents the cardinality of the set of all natural numbers

annotations.note: `Cardinal.aleph0` (`ℵ₀`) is the cardinality of ℕ.

annotations.label: Ordinal numbers

anchor.snippet: Ordinal numbers generalize the notion of order to infinite sets

annotations.note: `Ordinal` is the type of order types of well-orders.

annotations.label: Omega (smallest infinite ordinal)

anchor.snippet: the desired property of being the smallest ordinal greater than all finite ordinal numbers

annotations.note: `Ordinal.omega0` (`ω`) is the order type of ℕ, the least infinite ordinal.

annotations.label: First uncountable ordinal (ω₁)

anchor.snippet: is the set of all countable ordinals

annotations.note: `ω₁` is notation for `Ordinal.omega 1`, the first uncountable ordinal.

annotations.label: Successor cardinal

anchor.snippet: is the set of all ordinals with cardinality up to

annotations.note: `aleph_succ : ℵ_(succ o) = succ (ℵ_ o)` is the successor cardinal via `Order.succ`.

annotations.label: Hartogs' theorem

anchor.snippet: Proving that such a set always exists is known as Hartogs' theorem

annotations.note: Hartogs is not named, but its consequence (every cardinal has a strictly larger successor aleph) is formalized via `aleph_succ`/`range_aleph`.

annotations.label: No cardinality strictly between consecutive alephs

anchor.snippet: there cannot exist any set with cardinality between

annotations.note: Since `ℵ_(succ o) = succ (ℵ_ o)`, nothing lies strictly between an aleph and the next by `Order.succ` semantics.

annotations.label: Every infinite set has cardinality of some aleph

anchor.snippet: every infinite set has some cardinality corresponding uniquely to some aleph

annotations.note: `mem_range_aleph_iff : c ∈ range aleph ↔ ℵ₀ ≤ c` says every infinite cardinal is an aleph.

annotations.label: Cardinal addition

anchor.snippet: cardinal addition is defined as

annotations.note: `add_def : #α + #β = #(α ⊕ β)` defines cardinal addition as disjoint union.

annotations.label: Cardinal multiplication

anchor.snippet: cardinal multiplication is defined as

annotations.note: `mul_def` defines cardinal multiplication as the cardinality of the product type.

annotations.label: Associativity of cardinal arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Associativity

annotations.note: `Cardinal` is a commutative semiring, so `+` and `*` are associative.

annotations.label: Commutativity of cardinal arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Commutativity

annotations.note: `Cardinal`'s commutative semiring instance gives `add_comm` and `mul_comm`.

annotations.label: Distributivity of cardinal arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Distributivity

annotations.note: Distributivity `mul_add` holds from the commutative semiring structure on `Cardinal`.

annotations.label: Kőnig's theorem

anchor.snippet: strict inequalities (such as Kőnig's theorem ) are rare

annotations.note: `Cardinal.sum_lt_prod` is König's theorem on cardinals.

annotations.label: κ + κ = κ for infinite cardinals

anchor.snippet: In fact, for any infinite cardinal

annotations.note: `add_eq_self : ℵ₀ ≤ c → c + c = c`.

annotations.label: Cardinal exponentiation

anchor.snippet: is defined via set exponentiation

annotations.note: The `Pow` instance with `mk_arrow : #(α → β) = #β ^ #α` defines cardinal exponentiation as function sets.

annotations.label: Zero to the power of zero is one

anchor.snippet: includes as a corollary that zero to the power of zero is one

annotations.note: `power_zero : a ^ 0 = 1` gives `0 ^ 0 = 1` for cardinals.

annotations.label: 2^κ equals cardinality of the power set

anchor.snippet: by considering the indicator function of each subset

annotations.note: `mk_set : #(Set α) = 2 ^ #α` is the power-set cardinality via indicator functions.

annotations.label: Cardinality of continuum is some aleph (which is unprovable)

anchor.snippet: it is unprovable from standard set theories which aleph it corresponds to

annotations.note: That 𝔠 is some aleph follows from `mem_range_aleph_iff`, but the independence (which aleph) is not formalized.

annotations.label: No set of all cardinal numbers (proper class)

anchor.snippet: Such a set cannot exist, which has been considered paradoxical, and related to the Burali-Forti paradox

annotations.note: `not_small_cardinal : ¬ Small.{u} Cardinal` says the cardinals form a proper class.

annotations.label: Continuum (real numbers)

anchor.snippet: formalize the intuitive notion of the continuum

annotations.note: `Real` (ℝ) is the completion of ℚ formalizing the continuum.

annotations.label: Cardinality of the continuum (𝔠)

anchor.snippet: turns out to be remarkably stable under various transformations

annotations.note: `Cardinal.continuum` (`𝔠`) is defined as `2 ^ ℵ₀`.

annotations.label: All intervals have the same cardinality as ℝ

anchor.snippet: all intervals on the real line

annotations.note: `mk_Ioo_real`/`mk_Icc_real` etc. show every nondegenerate interval has cardinality 𝔠 = #ℝ.

annotations.label: Tangent function bijection to ℝ

anchor.snippet: the tangent function is a bijection from the interval

annotations.note: `Real.tanOrderIso : Ioo (-(π/2)) (π/2) ≃o ℝ` is the tangent bijection.

annotations.label: Cantor set

anchor.snippet: A more surprising example is the Cantor set , which is defined as follows

annotations.note: `cantorSet` is the standard ternary Cantor set in ℝ.

annotations.label: Bijection between a line and a square

anchor.snippet: one can find a bijective map between a one-dimensional line and a two-dimensional square

annotations.note: `mul_eq_self` gives 𝔠·𝔠 = 𝔠 so #(ℝ×ℝ) = #ℝ; no explicit named line↔square bijection is provided.

annotations.label: Space-filling curves (Peano, Hilbert)

anchor.snippet: Space-filling curves offer a more visual proof

annotations.note: Peano/Hilbert space-filling curves are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: ℝⁿ has the same cardinality as ℝ

anchor.snippet: Both of these methods can be reused at each dimension

annotations.note: Provable via cardinal arithmetic (𝔠ⁿ = 𝔠) but there is no dedicated `#(Fin n → ℝ) = 𝔠` lemma.

annotations.label: Countable cartesian product equinumerous to ℝ

anchor.snippet: can also be shown to be equinumerous to

annotations.note: `continuum_power_aleph0 : 𝔠 ^ ℵ₀ = 𝔠`, i.e. a countable product of continua is again 𝔠.

annotations.label: ℝ strictly larger than ℕ

anchor.snippet: the set of real numbers is strictly larger than the set of natural numbers

annotations.note: `aleph0_lt_continuum : ℵ₀ < 𝔠` together with `mk_real` gives #ℕ < #ℝ.

annotations.label: Continuum Hypothesis

anchor.snippet: The Continuum Hypothesis (CH) asserts that the real numbers have the next largest cardinality after the natural numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define the Continuum Hypothesis as a statement.

annotations.label: Independence of CH from ZFC

anchor.snippet: As shown by Gödel and Cohen , the continuum hypothesis is independent of ZFC

annotations.note: Independence of CH (forcing/constructibility) is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalized Continuum Hypothesis

anchor.snippet: The Generalized Continuum Hypothesis (GCH) extends this to all infinite cardinals

annotations.note: GCH is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Beth numbers

anchor.snippet: provide a concise notation for powersets of the real numbers

annotations.note: `Cardinal.beth` is the beth function with `beth (succ o) = 2 ^ beth o`.

annotations.label: Axiom of Infinity

anchor.snippet: the Axiom of Infinity , stating roughly "there is an infinite set"

annotations.note: In Mathlib's ZFC model `ZFSet.omega` (with `omega_zero`/`omega_succ`) realizes an infinite set.

annotations.label: Axiom of power set

anchor.snippet: the Axiom of power set , which says that, for any set

annotations.note: `ZFSet.powerset` with `mem_powerset` realizes the power-set axiom in the ZFC model.

annotations.label: Axiom of Choice

anchor.snippet: it states that given any collection of non-empty sets, it is possible to construct a new set by choosing one element from each set

annotations.note: `ZFSet.choice` (and Lean's `Classical.axiomOfChoice`) provides a choice function for collections of nonempty sets.

annotations.label: Without AC: surjection without injection

anchor.snippet: there exists sets, such that there is a surjection from

annotations.note: Choice-free consistency results of this kind are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Without AC: incomparable sets (trichotomy fails)

anchor.snippet: there exist incomparable sets under cardinal inequality

annotations.note: The failure of cardinal trichotomy in ZF is not formalized (Mathlib works with choice).

annotations.label: Trichotomy and surjection⇒injection equivalent to AC

anchor.snippet: Both of these (trichotomy and that surjection implies injection) are equivalent to AC

annotations.note: Equivalences with AC over ZF are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Representative cardinality function impossible without AC

anchor.snippet: is impossible without AC

annotations.note: No metatheorem about choice-free cardinal representatives is formalized.

annotations.label: Frege–Russell–Scott definition (Scott's trick)

anchor.snippet: adopting the so-called Frege–Russell–Scott definition, introduced by Dana Scott

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Cardinal` uses a type-quotient, not Scott's trick of minimal-rank equivalence classes.

annotations.label: Multiplicative axiom equivalent to AC

anchor.snippet: the arbitrary product of non-zero cardinal numbers always non-zero—called the Multiplicative axiom by Bertrand Russell—are both equivalent to AC

annotations.note: The equivalence of the multiplicative axiom with AC is not formalized.

annotations.label: ZF+GCH derives AC

anchor.snippet: it can be shown ZF+GCH is sufficient to derive AC

annotations.note: The Sierpiński result (GCH ⇒ AC) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Proper class

anchor.snippet: A class is called a proper class when, at least intuitively, it is "too large" to form a set

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Class` (a collection that need not be a set) and the set/non-set distinction, though no single `IsProper` predicate.

annotations.label: Examples of proper classes

anchor.snippet: the Universe of all sets , the class of all cardinal numbers , and the class of all ordinal numbers are proper classes

annotations.note: The universal class `Class.univ` exists, and `not_small_cardinal`/non-smallness of `Ordinal` express that cardinals and ordinals form proper classes.

annotations.label: Axiom of limitation of size

anchor.snippet: he defined a class to be "too big" (a proper class) if and only if it was equinumerous with the whole Universe of sets

annotations.note: Von Neumann's axiom of limitation of size is not formalized.

annotations.label: All proper classes have the same size

anchor.snippet: Thus, all proper classes have the same "size"

annotations.note: No theorem stating all proper classes are equinumerous is formalized.

annotations.label: Large cardinal axioms

anchor.snippet: Large cardinal axioms assert the existence of cardinal numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes specific large cardinals (e.g. `IsInaccessible`) but not a general large-cardinal-axiom framework.

annotations.label: Inaccessible cardinal

anchor.snippet: an inaccessible cardinal is, roughly, a cardinal that cannot be approached from below using basic set-theoretic operations

annotations.note: `IsInaccessible c` means `c` is an uncountable regular strong-limit cardinal.

annotations.label: Von Neumann hierarchy

anchor.snippet: Large cardinals are understood in terms of the Von Neumann hierarchy

annotations.note: `ZFSet.vonNeumann o` (`V_ o`) is the cumulative Von Neumann hierarchy with `V_(o+1) = powerset (V_ o)`.

annotations.label: Inaccessible cardinal implies consistency of ZFC

anchor.snippet: implies the consistency of ZFC, since if

annotations.note: Consistency strength of inaccessibles over ZFC is not formalized.

annotations.label: Measurable cardinal inconsistent with constructibility

anchor.snippet: the existence of a measurable cardinal is inconsistent with Gödel's constructibility axiom

annotations.note: Measurable cardinals and the constructible universe L are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reinhardt cardinal inconsistent with AC

anchor.snippet: the existence of a Reinhardt cardinal is inconsistent with the axiom of choice

annotations.note: Reinhardt cardinals and the Kunen inconsistency are not formalized.

annotations.label: Axiom of Determinacy

anchor.snippet: The Axiom of Determinacy (AD) asserts that certain kinds of mathematical games on the natural numbers are determined

annotations.note: The Axiom of Determinacy is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: AD implies regularity properties of reals

anchor.snippet: it implies the perfect set property , the property of Baire , and that all sets of real numbers are Lebesgue measurable

annotations.note: Consequences of AD are not formalized.

annotations.label: AD inconsistent with AC

anchor.snippet: it was shown that this axiom is inconsistent with AC

annotations.note: The inconsistency of AD with AC is not formalized.

annotations.label: Banach–Tarski paradox

anchor.snippet: The Banach–Tarski paradox is a paradox arising from AC which allows one to disassemble a ball into finitely many pieces

annotations.note: The Banach–Tarski paradox is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Partition of ℝ with more parts than reals

anchor.snippet: It is possible to partition the set of real numbers in such a way that there are strictly more nonempty sets of real numbers than there are real numbers

annotations.note: This choice-free partition phenomenon is not formalized.

annotations.label: Löwenheim–Skolem theorem

anchor.snippet: the Löwenheim–Skolem theorem states that if a theory has an infinite model, then it also has models of every other infinite cardinality

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the Downward Löwenheim–Skolem theorem; the upward direction is not present.

annotations.label: Skolem's paradox

anchor.snippet: Thus, Skolem's paradox was posed as follows

annotations.note: Skolem's paradox about countable models of set theory is not formalized.

annotations.label: Consistent first-order set theory has a countable model

anchor.snippet: any first-order set theory, if it is consistent , has a model which is countable

annotations.note: Existence of countable models of set theory (completeness applied to ZF) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Löwenheim–Skolem fails in higher-order logic

anchor.snippet: the Löwenheim–Skolem theorem does not hold

annotations.note: Higher-order failure of Löwenheim–Skolem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Euclidean principle (whole greater than part)

anchor.snippet: the fifth common notion states "The whole is greater than the part", called the Euclidean principle

annotations.note: `Finset.card_lt_card : s ⊂ t → s.card < t.card` is the whole-greater-than-part principle for finite sets.

annotations.label: Aristotle's wheel paradox

anchor.snippet: known as Aristotle's wheel paradox

annotations.note: A historical paradox with no formal counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Galileo's paradox

anchor.snippet: Galileo Galilei presented what was later coined Galileo's paradox

annotations.note: The paradox itself (squares vs. naturals) is not formalized as a statement.

annotations.label: Hume's principle

anchor.snippet: now called Hume's principle

annotations.note: `Cardinal.eq : #α = #β ↔ Nonempty (α ≃ β)` is Hume's principle.

annotations.label: Bolzano's pairing of intervals

anchor.snippet: He discussed examples such as the pairing between the intervals

annotations.note: Mathlib shows any two nondegenerate intervals have equal cardinality 𝔠, though Bolzano's explicit linear pairing is not named.
